title: Isaiah
author: Isaiah son of Amoz; critical scholarship posits two or three authors (chs. 1–39 and 40–66), though the NT consistently attributes the whole to Isaiah
date: ~740–700 BC (Isaiah's ministry); possibly extending to 680 BC
setting: Judah under kings Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah — a period of Assyrian threat, internal corruption, and spiritual declension. Isaiah ministers as the Assyrian empire rises and Jerusalem is miraculously delivered.
purpose: To call Judah to covenant faithfulness, to announce the coming judgment of exile, and — uniquely among the prophets — to set this judgment in the context of stunning promises of redemption, the Servant of the LORD, and a new creation.
themes: The holiness of God ('the Holy One of Israel' — 25x)
Judgment on Judah, Israel, and the nations
The Suffering Servant as the atonement for sin
The new exodus and return from exile
The new creation: new heavens and new earth

outline.label: Book of Judgment (Prophecies to Israel and Nations)
The Book of Comfort — Return from Exile
The New Community and New Creation

before.label: Amos and Hosea preach judgment in the northern kingdom
Uzziah's death — the stable era of Judah's prosperity ends
Fall of Samaria — northern Israel exiled to Assyria

after.label: Sennacherib's siege of Jerusalem; Hezekiah's prayer
Jeremiah — the fall of Jerusalem and exile to Babylon
Cyrus's decree of return — Isaiah's prophecy fulfilled

key_people.name: Isaiah ben Amoz
key_people.role: Jerusalem prophet active 740–700 BC; his vision of the Holy One of Israel (ch. 6) shapes his entire ministry and gives the book its central theological designation for God

key_people.name: Ahaz
key_people.role: King of Judah who refuses to trust God in the Syro-Ephraimite crisis (ch. 7), providing the occasion for the Immanuel prophecy — a landmark in messianic prediction

key_people.name: Hezekiah
key_people.role: The faithful king who prays and receives deliverance from Sennacherib (chs. 36–39), embodying the trust the book calls for, yet whose pride in showing his treasures to Babylon foreshadows the exile

key_people.name: Cyrus
key_people.role: The Persian king named by Isaiah over a century before his birth (44:28–45:1) as the one who will authorize the return from exile — the most specific predictive prophecy in the OT

key_people.name: The Servant of the LORD
key_people.role: The mysterious figure of the Servant Songs (42; 49; 50; 52–53) who suffers vicariously for his people's sins — the most direct OT anticipation of Christ's atoning work

context: Isaiah ministered in Jerusalem from Uzziah's death (740 BC) through Sennacherib's siege (701 BC). The book's scope extends prophetically to the Babylonian exile and return (chs. 40–55) and to the ultimate eschatological restoration (chs. 56–66). Isaiah is the most quoted OT book in the NT (over 400 citations or allusions), shaped by the twin crises of the Assyrian threat (chs. 1–39) and the anticipated Babylonian exile (chs. 40–66).
christ_connection: Isaiah is the preeminent OT witness to Jesus Christ. The Servant Songs (42:1–9; 49:1–13; 50:4–11; 52:13–53:12) describe his ministry, suffering, and atoning death with such precision that early Christians called Isaiah 'the fifth gospel.' Specific fulfilments include: Immanuel born of a virgin (7:14; Matt 1:23), Galilee as the scene of his ministry (9:1–2; Matt 4:15–16), riding into Jerusalem humbly (62:11; Matt 21:5), and 'by his wounds we are healed' (53:5; 1 Pet 2:24). The New Exodus themes of chapters 40–55 are fulfilled in Christ's redemption.

key_verses.note: The seraphim's declaration 'Holy, holy, holy is the LORD of hosts' — the trisagion that establishes God's absolute holiness as the foundation of Isaiah's entire ministry and message
'Behold, the virgin shall conceive and bear a son, and shall call his name Immanuel' — the sign given to Ahaz that the New Testament applies to Jesus' birth (Matt 1:23)
'They who wait for the LORD shall renew their strength... they shall mount up with wings like eagles' — the pastoral heart of chapters 40–55, addressing exiles with the promise that God is not weary
'He was pierced for our transgressions... and by his wounds we are healed' — the most explicit OT statement of vicarious atonement, applied to Jesus throughout the NT

themes_detail.title: The Holiness of God and Human Uncleanness
themes_detail.text: Isaiah's inaugural vision (ch. 6) — the Lord enthroned, the seraphim covering their faces, the smoke and fire, the prophet's undone cry — establishes the controlling theological reality of the book: God is holy in a way that unmakes human pretension. The refrain 'the Holy One of Israel' (occurring 26 times, more than in the rest of the OT combined) is Isaiah's signature theological contribution: YHWH's holiness is not abstract perfection but a dynamic, demanding, life-transforming reality that both judges and redeems.

themes_detail.title: Judgment and Salvation as Two Sides of One Covenant
themes_detail.text: Isaiah resists any reading that divides the book into a gloomy first half (judgment) and a hopeful second half (salvation). Both themes are present throughout, because they flow from the same God who is both Judge and Savior. Judgment is God's alien work — strange to him but necessary to clear the ground for restoration. Salvation is his proper work — the goal toward which judgment aims. The structure of Isaiah's call (6:9–13) embeds this paradox: preach until the people are deaf, yet a holy seed will remain.

themes_detail.title: The Servant of the LORD
themes_detail.text: The four Servant Songs (42:1–9; 49:1–13; 50:4–11; 52:13–53:12) develop an enigmatic figure whose identity shifts between Israel (as God's servant in history) and an individual who suffers on Israel's behalf. The fourth song (52:13–53:12) is the most searching: the Servant is despised, stricken, pierced — yet his suffering is explicitly substitutionary ('for our transgressions... for our iniquities') and ultimately vindicatory ('he shall see his offspring'). The New Testament's identification of this Servant with Jesus is not eisegesis but the text's own trajectory.

themes_detail.title: The New Exodus and Universal Redemption
themes_detail.text: Isaiah 40–55 is structured as a second Exodus: just as YHWH redeemed Israel from Egypt, he will now redeem his people from Babylon — through a new wilderness journey, with a new way prepared, and a new song to sing. But this new redemption is explicitly universal in scope (52:10, 'all the ends of the earth shall see the salvation of our God'), incorporating the nations into the people of God. The servant's mission includes bringing justice to the nations and being 'a light for the nations' (42:6) — language that Paul applies to his own Gentile mission (Acts 13:47).
